title: practical english usage - Part 109
content:
Nobody except his best friends like him. (More correct: Nobody... likes him.) sometimes use a plural verb. This is not usually considered correct.

When  a singular subject is modified by a following plural expression, people 3 singular      subject,     plural     continuation,         plural     verb For singular and plural verbs after interrogative what and who, ►     130.5.

What we need is/are a few bright young engineers.

What I am most interested in is/are your immediate personal reactions.

when the complement is long.

This often happens, too, when the subject is a relative what-clause, especially weekend, without any doubt, was/were the tennis championships.

The most interesting thing on radio and television last grammar •  130   mixed singular and plural: other structures The tallest plants grow to about 1.75 feet.

not                                           m It weighs one and a half tons, (     ... one^anda half   ) Fractions between 1 and 2 are normally used with plural nouns (►  322.2).

I go to Ireland every six weeks.

expressions in measurements of frequency.

Every (which is normally used with singular nouns) can be used before plural 9 every     (frequency) I don't like those kind of boots.

demonstratives with kind, sort or type. For details, ►    592.

In an informal style, we  sometimes mix singular and plural forms when we  use 8  kind,     sort     and   type I bet that house could tell a good few stories.

I've lain awake a good many nights worrying about you.

Note also the expression a good many J few + plural (informal).

I've had a very busy three days.

She spent a happy ten minutes looking through the photos.

He's been waiting for a good two hours.

We'll need an extra ten pounds.

I want to stay for another three weeks.

a/an + adjective.

Plural expressions of quantity can be used with another (►  550) and with 7 another,         a/an    + adjective Where's those books I lent you?

Here's your keys.   There's some children at the door.

In an informal style, here's, there's and where's are common with plural nouns.

6 here's,      there's       and   where's What we need most of all are some really new ideas.

especially if what is a long way from the verb.

However, plural verbs are often used before longer plural complements, What she needs is friends. (More natural than What she needs are friends.) Relative   /-clauses are normally the subject of a singular verb.

'What are your politics? '(My politics are) extreme left-wing.' 'Who are your closest friends? '(My closest friends are) Naomi and Bridget.’ complement of a clause, the verb with who or what can be plural.

With verbs like be, where the answer to a who or what question is the not 'What lives in those little holes? 'Rabbits (do), (     What live .. .)' Who was at the party? (More natural than Who were at the party?) tomorrow).' (More natural than Who are working tomorrow?) 'Who is working tomorrow? 'Jake, Lucy and Shareena (are working who or what is usually singular, even when the answer is plural.

In a who or what question, if the answer is the subject of a clause, the verb with 5 who     and   what Section11 Nouns     and   Noun    Phrases;    Agreement grammar •  131    distributive plural: Tell them to bring raincoats.

Discourse markers often come at the beginning of sentences.

This often happens with fixed singular expressions like at the beginning.

Children may resemble both their father and their mother in different ways.

Subjects agree with their verb.

Mixtures of singular and plural are possible.

(   All documents must be accompanied by translations of the originals.) All documents must be accompanied by a translation of the original.

perfect verb forms.) or We use a past participle in a perfect verb form. (   We use past participles in In generalisations and rules, singular and plural nouns are both possible.

3 generalisations           and   rules He's not at his best in the morning(s).

We usually go and see my mother on Saturday(s).

often possible with little difference of meaning.
